title: Te Aho Tapu o Te Awa: Weaving Stories of Mangakōtukutuku Stream / The Sacred Thread of the Stream: Weaving Stories of Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the stream where the bellbird gathers), learning about its role in Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau (stories), its connection to local food gathering practices, and how contemporary artists respond to environmental changes affecting our awa.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three ways Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku is represented in Ngāti Kahungunu visual arts and storytelling
Create an original artwork that responds to the cultural and environmental significance of our local awa using both traditional and contemporary materials
Collaboratively develop and present a proposal for an art installation that addresses an environmental issue affecting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Articulate personal connections to place through reflective artist statements that incorporate te reo Māori place names and concepts

activities.title: Whakarongo ki te Awa: Listening to the Stream
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua to hear pūrākau (traditional stories) of the stream, learn its original Māori name meanings, and observe the current state of the waterway through sketching and sound recording.

activities.title: Toi Awa: Traditional and Contemporary Art Exploration
activities.description: Students examine historical and contemporary artworks inspired by waterways from local Ngāti Kahungunu artists, then experiment with creating their own water-based pigments using natural materials from around the stream.

activities.title: Te Auahatanga: Creative Response to Environmental Challenge
activities.description: Working with local environmental scientists, students identify one issue affecting Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (e.g., sediment runoff, pollution) and brainstorm how art could raise awareness or propose solutions to this challenge.

activities.title: Whakairo Kōrero: Story Weaving Through Mixed Media
activities.description: Students create individual mixed-media artworks that combine traditional patterns (kowhaiwhai), contemporary materials, and text to tell a story about their personal connection to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga: Community Exhibition Preparation
activities.description: Students curate a classroom exhibition of their artworks, writing bilingual labels and artist statements, and preparing to present their work and environmental proposals to whānau and community members.

resources: Local kaumātua and knowledge holders from Ngāti Kahungunu
Hawke's Bay Cultural Trust's online archive of local Māori artists
Traditional natural dye materials (kōkōwai, paru) and contemporary art supplies
Water testing kits and environmental monitoring data from Hawke's Bay Regional Council

tasks: Completed mixed-media artwork responding to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Written artist statement incorporating te reo Māori concepts
Group presentation proposing an art-based environmental solution
Participation in final community exhibition and reflection

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Level 4 - Investigate the purpose of objects and images from past and present cultures and identify the contexts in which they were or are made, viewed, and valued
Developing Practical Knowledge in the Arts: Level 4 - Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and selected principles through the use of materials and processes
Developing Ideas in the Arts: Level 4 - Develop and refine ideas in selected art disciplines in response to a variety of motivations, using imagination and a range of sources

Communicating and Interpreting in the Arts: Level 4 - Prepare and present brief drama, dance, music, or visual arts performances and describe how the purposes of the works are expressed through their presentation
